g.data-narration: Welcome to SIGCLD Semantics in Unix/Linux. This lecture covers process management, zombie states, and proper signal handling.
SIGCLD (Signal Child) is sent to the parent when a child process terminates. It's essentially an alias for SIGCHLD in modern Linux systems.
g.text: Signal sent to parent when a child terminates or when wait() is called.
Default Action: IGNORE - leads to zombies if wait() not called!

g.data-narration: A Unix process can be in Running, Sleeping, Stopped, or Zombie state. Understanding these states is essential for proper process management.
A zombie process has terminated but remains in the process table until the parent reads its exit status via wait(). Without proper handling, zombies accumulate and exhaust system resources.
g.text: A zombie is a terminated process that remains in the process table.
The kernel keeps exit status for the parent to retrieve via wait().
If parent never calls wait() → zombies accumulate → system instability!
Orphaned zombie: Parent dies first → init (PID 1) adopts and reaps it.

g.data-narration: The wait() system call blocks until any child terminates. It returns the child's PID and stores exit status. Call wait() to prevent zombie accumulation.
waitpid() provides flexible control - specify which child to wait for, use WNOHANG for non-blocking behavior. Essential for event-driven programs.
g.text: The waitpid() System Call - More Control
pid_t waitpid(pid_t pid, int *status, int options);
WNOHANG: Don't block, return 0 if no child exited

g.data-narration: Use WIFEXITED to check normal exit, WIFSIGNALED for signal kill, WIFSTOPPED for suspended processes. Always check WIFEXITED before WEXITSTATUS.
g.text: ⚠ Always check WIFEXITED before WEXITSTATUS! Status contains garbage if killed by signal.

g.data-narration: Install a signal handler using signal() or sigaction() to handle SIGCLD asynchronously. This allows the parent to be notified when children terminate.
The sigaction() function is the POSIX-recommended way to install signal handlers. It provides more control and portability than signal().
g.text: int sigaction(int sig, const struct sigaction *act,
• sa_mask: signals to block during handler

g.data-narration: In signal handlers, always use waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in a loop. Multiple children may terminate before the handler executes.
g.text: Signal Handler Best Practice - WNOHANG Loop

g.data-narration: The SA_RESTART flag in sigaction() causes interrupted system calls to automatically restart. This prevents EINTR errors in blocking I/O operations.
g.text: SA_RESTART Flag - Automatic System Call Restart
• No need to handle EINTR manually

g.data-narration: This basic example shows fork(), child doing work, parent blocking on wait() to retrieve exit status. No zombie remains after wait().
This example shows async handling - parent creates children, continues work. SIGCLD handler automatically reaps terminated children without blocking.
In summary: SIGCLD signals child termination, always call wait() to prevent zombies, use waitpid(WNOHANG) in handlers, and prefer sigaction() for portable code.
Avoid these mistakes: calling blocking wait() in handlers, not checking WIFEXITED, forgetting SA_RESTART, and not reaping children leading to zombie accumulation.
g.text: ✗ Using wait() inside signal handler (can block!)
✗ Using WEXITSTATUS without checking WIFEXITED first
✗ Not calling wait()/waitpid() - leading to zombie accumulation
✓ Always use waitpid(-1, &status, WNOHANG) in signal handlers
